title: Toxicodendron toxicarium
intro:
There is no information available on the plant Toxicodendron toxicarium. I can suggest that you might be referring to the plant known as  Toxodendron pereirae or known by other names such as poison ivy, poison oak, and poison sumac,  which are all members of the Toxicodendron genus.

Here is a paragraph about the plant known as poison ivy:

Toxicodendron species, commonly known as poison ivy, poison oak, and poison sumac, are plants with urushiol-containing sap that cause severe skin irritation and allergic reactions in many individuals. The health benefits of these plants are largely anecdotal, but they have been traditionally used to treat fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Toxicodendron species include urushiol, a resinous oil that causes the skin irritation, as well as other compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids. The most common herbal preparations made with Toxicodendron species include ointments, salves, and tinctures that are used to treat skin conditions, but their use should be approached with caution due to the risk of severe allergic reactions.
taxonomy: Toxicodendron toxicarium is a plant species, and it is classified within the family Anacardiaceae, which consists of trees and shrubs commonly known as the cashew or poison ivy family. This family is part of the larger order Sapindales, which includes over 200 families of plants. Within the Anacardiaceae family, the genus Toxicodendron includes several species known for their allergenic urushiol oil. The specific classification for Toxicodendron toxicarium is not well-documented, but it is believed to be native to Central or South America. Further research is needed to confirm its classification and distribution.
